$comment: Needfire emergency protocols. Hand-derived from the seed corpus documents named in source_doc — every step must be traceable to its source. Schema: Protocol{id,title,short,icon,severity(life-threat|urgent|serious),keywords[],source_doc,entry_warning?,disclaimer,start,steps[]}; Step{id,type(action|decision|loop|info),text(markdown),detail?,warning?,timer?,options?(decision only, 2-4),next?(id|END|omitted=next in array),loop{until,back_to}?}; Timer{kind(metronome|countdown|stamp),label,bpm?,seconds?,count?,persist?,auto?(never true for metronome)}.

protocols.title: CPR — Adult, Child, Infant
protocols.entry_warning: Send someone for help and an AED NOW.
protocols.disclaimer: If you can call your local emergency number (911 / 112), do it now. General reference only, not a substitute for trained medical care — hand over to a trained responder as soon as one is available.

steps.text: **Check response.** Shout and tap the shoulders. Look for normal breathing — no more than 10 seconds. **Gasping is NOT normal breathing.**
**Call it: what are you seeing?**
They do not need CPR. Lay them on their **side** (recovery position), keep the airway clear, keep them warm, and **watch their breathing continuously**.
**Who is the patient?** This changes hand position and depth.
**If you are alone:** go get help and an AED NOW, then come back and start compressions. If someone else is here, they go — you start compressions immediately.
If you are alone with a child or infant, **do CPR for about 2 minutes BEFORE leaving them** to get help.

**Compress the centre of the chest, hard and fast.** Firm surface. Rate **100–120/min**. Let the chest recoil fully; minimise pauses.

- **Adult:** two hands, 5–6 cm (2 in) deep
- **Child:** one or two hands, about 5 cm
- **Infant:** two fingers (or two thumbs, hands encircling), about 4 cm
steps.detail: Push with straight arms, shoulders over hands, using your body weight. Cracked ribs happen — keep going.

steps.text: **Are you trained in rescue breaths?** Breaths matter more for children, infants, and drowning victims.
**30 compressions, then 2 breaths.** Tilt head, lift chin. Cover an infant's mouth AND nose. The metronome counter rolls over at 30.

loop.until: Signs of life, an AED tells you to stand clear, trained help takes over, or you physically cannot continue. Swap rescuers every 2 minutes.

steps.text: **Continuous compressions.** Do not stop to check. Swap rescuers every 2 minutes if you can.

loop.until: Signs of life, an AED tells you to stand clear, trained help takes over, or you physically cannot continue.

steps.text: **When an AED arrives:** attach it immediately, turn it on, follow its voice prompts. **Resume compressions immediately after any shock.**
steps.warning: Do not stop CPR to wait for the AED — keep compressing until the pads are on and the machine says stand clear.

steps.text: **Off-grid: when is it reasonable to stop?** Keep going until they show signs of life (movement, breathing, coughing), an AED tells you to stand clear, someone can take over, or you are physically unable to continue.
steps.detail: Without a defibrillator or advanced care, survival is very low once there are **no signs of life after 20–30 minutes** of good CPR. If you are exhausted, alone, in danger, or help will truly never come, it is acceptable — and not a failure — to stop. **Keep going far longer** if the person is **cold/hypothermic, was pulled from cold water, was buried in an avalanche, or was struck by lightning** — these can recover after prolonged CPR. The saying is 'not dead until warm and dead.'

protocols.title: Severe Bleeding
protocols.entry_warning: Severe bleeding can be fatal in minutes. Act immediately.
protocols.disclaimer: If you can call your local emergency number (911 / 112), do it now. General reference only, not a substitute for trained medical care — hand over to a trained responder as soon as one is available.

steps.text: **Press HARD on the wound, right now.** Use a clean cloth or your bare hand. Put your weight into it and **do not lift off to look** — constant pressure is what clots the blood.
steps.detail: Kneel and use both hands and your body weight. Any cloth works — a shirt, a scarf. Protect yourself from blood contact (gloves, a plastic bag) if you can grab it in a second, but do not delay a spurting bleed to find a barrier. Hold pressure for a full 10 minutes before you even think about easing off.

steps.text: **Where is the serious bleeding?** It decides whether a tourniquet is an option.
**Pack a deep or gaping wound and press.** Push clean gauze or cloth firmly INTO the wound, down onto the bleeding point, then press hard on top for a solid 10 minutes.
steps.detail: Keep stuffing more material in if it fills with blood — do not pull the packing back out. Raising the limb can help a little, but pressure is what stops it. If a fracture is likely, avoid moving the limb more than you must.

steps.text: **After a solid few minutes of hard pressure — is the bleeding controlled?**

**Apply a tourniquet — high and tight.** 5–7 cm (2–3 in) above the wound, **not over a joint**. Tighten until the bleeding STOPS and you cannot feel a pulse below it. It will hurt — that means it is working.
steps.warning: Once it is on and working, do NOT loosen it to peek. Write the time on their skin or forehead.
steps.detail:
- **No manufactured tourniquet?** Improvise: a band **at least 5 cm (2 in) wide** (a strip of cloth, a belt, a triangular bandage — never thin cord or wire, which cuts in) with a strong stick as a **windlass**. Tie the band, tie the stick over the knot, twist the stick to tighten until bleeding stops, then tie the stick in place so it can't unwind.
- **Still bleeding?** Put a **second tourniquet** just above the first and tighten it too.
- **Amputation or a mangled limb:** tourniquet and leave it — do not try to convert it.

steps.text: **Off-grid: help hours or days away?** A tourniquet is meant for about 2 hours. If it has been on **under ~2 hours**, the bleeding is fully controlled, and the limb is not mangled or amputated, you can try to **swap it for a packed pressure dressing** so the limb survives.
steps.detail: How to convert: pack the wound and put a firm pressure bandage on it, then **slowly loosen** the tourniquet (leave it in place, just loose) and watch closely. If bleeding restarts, **re-tighten it at once and leave it on** — do not try again. Do NOT attempt a conversion if you cannot watch the limb constantly, if the tourniquet has already been on more than ~6 hours, or if the person is in shock — in those cases leave it on and evacuate. Releasing a long-applied tourniquet without medical support can be dangerous.

steps.text: **Bandage firmly to hold the pressure.** Bind the packing/dressing tightly enough to keep pressure on the wound. If blood soaks through, **do NOT remove it — add more on top** and keep pressing.

**Neck, armpit, and groin can't take a tourniquet — pack them HARD.** Shove gauze or clean cloth right into the wound, onto the bleeding point, and press with your full body weight. Keep pushing more packing in.
steps.warning: Never tie anything around the neck. For the neck, press directly on the wound only.

loop.until: the bleeding is controlled, or trained help takes over

steps.text: **You cannot stop bleeding inside the chest or belly with pressure.** Keep the person still and flat, treat for shock, and get moving toward help. Cover any open wound.
steps.detail:
- **Chest wound that bubbles or sucks air** ('sucking chest wound'): cover it with something airtight (plastic, tape wrapper) taped down on **three sides only**, leaving one edge open to vent.
- **Abdominal wound with organs showing:** do NOT push them back in — cover them with a clean cloth dampened with clean water and keep it moist.
- Internal bleeding (belly, pelvis, thigh) shows as shock with no obvious external blood — handle gently and evacuate.

steps.text: **Pack and press, then dress.** Pack the wound if it is deep, keep firm pressure at least 10 minutes, then bandage firmly. Scalp wounds bleed dramatically but usually stop with steady pressure.
**Treat for shock.** Lay them flat and raise the legs (if no leg/spine injury). Keep them WARM — cover them and insulate from the ground. Reassure them and keep watching breathing and response.
steps.detail: Signs of shock from blood loss: pale, cold, clammy, grey skin; a fast weak pulse; fast breathing; thirst; restlessness then drowsiness. If they are fully alert and have no belly wound and may not reach care for a long time, small sips of water or oral rehydration solution help — stop if they feel sick. Nothing by mouth if drowsy or a belly injury is possible.

steps.text: **Ongoing care off-grid.** Keep the dressing on and the part still. Watch for re-bleeding and, over the next days, for **infection** (spreading redness, swelling, heat, pus, fever). Anyone who needed a tourniquet or wound packing must be evacuated to real care.
steps.detail: Clean around the wound (not into a fresh clot), immobilise the limb, and keep it slightly raised. Change the outer dressing if soaked or dirty, but leave a clot-holding packing undisturbed. Ensure tetanus protection. See *Wound Care and Infection* for cleaning, closure, and infection management over the following days.

protocols.title: Choking / Blocked Airway
protocols.disclaimer: If you can call your local emergency number (911 / 112), do it now. General reference only, not a substitute for trained medical care — hand over to a trained responder as soon as one is available.

steps.text: **Can they cough, speak, or cry?** A person clutching the throat who can't make a sound has a fully blocked airway.

options.label: No — silent, can't breathe, going blue
It's ME — I'm choking and alone

steps.text: **Encourage them to keep coughing.** A forceful cough clears an airway better than anything you can do. Do NOT slap the back of, or thrust on, someone who is coughing effectively. Stay with them.

loop.until: the object comes out, or they can no longer cough, speak, or breathe

steps.text: **Who is choking?** It changes the technique.
**5 sharp back blows.** Lean them well forward. Strike firmly between the shoulder blades with the heel of your hand. Check the mouth after each — remove anything you can SEE.
steps.detail: For a **pregnant or very large person** you cannot reach around, use **chest thrusts** instead of abdominal thrusts: fist on the centre of the breastbone, pull sharply straight back.

steps.text: **5 abdominal thrusts (Heimlich).** Stand behind them, make a fist just above the navel, grasp it with your other hand, and pull sharply **inward and upward**.
steps.warning: Never do a blind finger sweep — it can push the object deeper. Only remove what you can see.

steps.text: **After 5 back blows and 5 thrusts — what now?**

**Infant: 5 back blows, then 5 chest thrusts.** Lay the infant face-down along your forearm, head lower than the body; give 5 back blows. Turn face-up; give 5 chest thrusts with two fingers on the breastbone (sharper and deeper than CPR). **No abdominal thrusts on an infant.**
**After the back blows and chest thrusts — what now?**

**Choking and alone:** give yourself abdominal thrusts. Make a fist above your navel, grasp it with your other hand, and thrust in and up — or **press your upper belly hard over a firm edge** (the back of a chair, a railing, a rock) with a quick upward shove. Repeat.
steps.detail: Keep trying — do not run for help until the airway is clear or you must. If you feel you are about to pass out, get to where someone might find you and, if you can, make noise.

steps.text: **Is it clearing?**

**Unconscious → lower them gently to the ground and start CPR.** Before each set of breaths, LOOK in the mouth and remove the object **only if you can see it**. The chest compressions themselves help force the blockage out.
Open the **CPR protocol** and follow it: [CPR — Adult, Child, Infant](#/emergency/cpr). Keep checking the mouth for the dislodged object at each breath.
**Airway cleared — well done.** Let them rest and recover; a sore throat or cough is normal for a while.
steps.detail: Anyone who **received abdominal thrusts** should be checked for internal injury when care is available. Seek help if they have ongoing trouble swallowing or breathing, a persistent cough, chest pain, or the feeling that something is still stuck — the object may have passed into the lung.

protocols.entry_warning: Silent and can't breathe = fully blocked airway. Act now.

protocols.title: Burns — Heat, Chemical, Electrical
protocols.entry_warning: Stop the burning first — get them away from the source.
protocols.disclaimer: If you can call your local emergency number (911 / 112), do it now. General reference only, not a substitute for trained medical care — hand over to a trained responder as soon as one is available.

steps.text: **Stop the burning.** Remove the person from the source. Smother flames — stop, drop, roll.
**What kind of burn?**
**Brush off any dry powder first, then flush with water for 20+ minutes.** Remove contaminated clothing; protect yourself.

**Cut the power BEFORE touching the casualty.** Electrical damage runs deep between entry and exit wounds even when the skin looks minor. **Watch for heart rhythm problems** — if they collapse or stop breathing normally, go to [CPR](#/emergency/cpr).
**Cool the burn with cool running water for 20 minutes.** Cooling helps even if started an hour after the burn.
steps.warning: No ice, no ice water — ice deepens the injury.

steps.text: **Remove rings, watches, and tight clothing early**, before swelling starts. Do not pull off clothing stuck to the burn.
**Cover with a clean non-stick dressing** or kitchen cling film laid loosely over the wound. Keep the person warm — large burns lose heat fast.
steps.warning: Do NOT pop blisters, or apply butter, toothpaste, oil, or grease — they trap heat and cause infection.

steps.text:
**Check for a critical burn.** The victim's own palm (with fingers) is about 1% of their body surface — count palms to estimate size. Any of these present?

- Burns to the **face, hands, feet, genitals**, or over joints
- **Airway burn** signs: singed nostrils, hoarse voice, soot in mouth
- More than **10% of body surface**
- **Painless, white, leathery, or charred** areas (full-thickness)
- Smoke inhalation

**Treat as life-threatening — evacuate to the highest care available.** While waiting: keep the wound covered, keep them warm, and give frequent sips of water or oral rehydration solution if they are awake and able to drink — large burns leak enormous amounts of fluid and cause shock.

Keep the dressing clean and the person warm. For any **large burn**, give frequent sips of water or oral rehydration solution to an awake person who can drink — large burns leak fluid and cause shock.

protocols.title: Anaphylaxis — Severe Allergic Reaction
protocols.entry_warning: Trouble breathing or throat swelling after a sting, food, or drug = act NOW.
protocols.disclaimer: If you can call your local emergency number (911 / 112), do it now. General reference only, not a substitute for trained medical care — hand over to a trained responder as soon as one is available.

steps.text: **Is this anaphylaxis?** Soon after a sting, food, or medicine, a severe allergic reaction shows as any of: swelling of the **lips, tongue, or throat**; a **hoarse voice, wheeze, or trouble breathing**; widespread **hives or flushing**; vomiting or belly cramps; **dizziness or collapse** (low blood pressure).
steps.warning: Trouble breathing or any throat/mouth swelling, or faintness, means a life-threatening reaction — act now.

steps.text: **Do you have adrenaline (an auto-injector / EpiPen, or a vial and syringe)?**

**Give adrenaline (epinephrine) NOW** into the muscle of the **outer mid-thigh**, through clothing if needed.

- **Adult: 0.3 mg** (auto-injector, or 0.3 mL of 1 mg/mL)
- **Child: 0.15 mg** (roughly under 25–30 kg / 55–66 lb)
steps.warning: Antihistamines are NOT a substitute for adrenaline — they do nothing for the airway or blood pressure. Never delay adrenaline to try them.
steps.detail: Adrenaline given early is very safe — when in doubt, give it. Hold the injector in the thigh for several seconds; massage the spot. Note the time. Keep a second dose within reach.

steps.text: **No adrenaline — do everything else, hard, and evacuate desperately.** There is no true substitute, but good positioning and airway care save lives while you get help.
steps.detail:
- **Position** (next step) is critical — done wrong it kills.
- If they have an **asthma-type wheeze and a reliever inhaler**, give it (up to 10 puffs) — it can ease the airways.
- **Antihistamines** (if you have them) help itching and hives only, not the airway or blood pressure — give but do not rely on them.
- Be ready to open the airway and give **rescue breaths / CPR** if breathing stops.
- Send for help / adrenaline the instant symptoms appear — every minute counts.

steps.text: **Position them — how are they right now?**
steps.warning: Do NOT let them stand or sit up suddenly, and do not walk them — a sudden upright move can cause fatal collapse when blood pressure is low.

steps.text: Let them **sit up** to ease breathing — but keep them from standing. Stay with them.
Put them in the **recovery position** (on their side) so the airway stays clear and vomit drains.
Lay her on her **left side** (takes the womb's weight off the big vein).
**Lie them flat and raise the legs** to keep blood going to the heart and brain.
**Reassess in 5 minutes.** If you have adrenaline and they are not clearly improving (or are worsening), **repeat the dose after 5–15 minutes**. Keep watching the airway and breathing.

timer.label: Reassess — repeat adrenaline if not improving

steps.detail: If breathing stops or they become unresponsive and are not breathing normally, start [CPR](#/emergency/cpr). Scrape out a bee stinger sideways (don't pinch it). A second adrenaline dose is common and expected — do not hold back if they are not improving.

steps.text: **Even after they recover, watch closely for at least 4–6 hours** — symptoms can come back hours later with no new exposure (a biphasic reaction), and longer after a severe attack. **Evacuate to medical care even if they seem fully recovered**, and especially if adrenaline was needed or unavailable.

protocols.title: Poisoning
protocols.entry_warning: Do NOT induce vomiting — for any poison. Make the scene safe first.
protocols.disclaimer: If you can call your local emergency number (911 / 112), do it now. General reference only, not a substitute for trained medical care — hand over to a trained responder as soon as one is available.

steps.text: **Make the scene safe before you touch anyone.** A poison that got one person can get you.
steps.detail:
- **Gas, smoke, or fumes:** do NOT walk into it. Open doors/windows from outside, cut the source (stove, engine, generator) if you can reach it safely, and let it clear. A rescuer who collapses is now a second patient.
- **Chemical spill / powder:** protect your skin and eyes — gloves, or hands wrapped in cloth or a bag.
- Move the person to fresh, clean air only if it is safe for you to do so.

steps.text: **Check the person. How are they right now?**
**Put them on their side (recovery position)** so vomit drains and the tongue can't block the airway. Tilt the head back, keep checking they are breathing.
steps.warning: A drowsy or unconscious person can vomit and choke. Never give anything by mouth, and never leave them on their back.

steps.text: **Not breathing normally = start CPR now.** Open the [CPR protocol](#/emergency/cpr) and follow it. Send someone to keep looking for the poison's container while you work.

**Protect them from injury** — clear hard objects away, cushion the head, do NOT restrain them or put anything in the mouth. Time it. See the [Seizure protocol](#/emergency/seizure). When it stops, put them on their side and go on.
**How did the poison get in?**
**Get them into fresh air** (only if safe for you). Loosen tight clothing. Keep them still and calm — exertion worsens it. If you have oxygen, give it.
steps.detail:
- **Carbon monoxide** (stove, heater, generator, engine, charcoal in an enclosed space) is invisible and has no smell. If everyone in the shelter has a headache, feels sick, or is dizzy, treat it as CO: get everyone out and ventilate before going back in. CO poisoning can need many hours of oxygen — evacuate toward help if you can.
- **Smoke from a fire** can carry cyanide as well as CO — same first aid, get out and get air.
- Some inhaled irritants (chlorine, ammonia, smoke) inflame the lungs **hours later** — watch breathing for 24 hours even if they seem fine.

steps.text: **Remove contaminated clothing** (protect your own hands). **Brush off any dry powder first**, then flush the skin with lots of clean water.
steps.detail:
- Flush for **15–20 minutes**, longer for a corrosive (lye, acid, oven/drain cleaner). Let the water run off and away, not onto clean skin.
- **Do not scrub** — it drives chemical in. Do not try to 'neutralise' with another chemical.
- Cover any chemical burn loosely and clean afterward. Keep the product name/label.

steps.text: **Flush the eye with clean water for 15–20 minutes**, lids held open, water running from the nose side outward so it doesn't cross to the other eye.
steps.detail: Alkalis (lye, cement, ammonia) keep burning — flush even longer. Remove contact lenses once flushing has started. See [Eye Injuries](#/read/eye-injuries.md). Do not let them rub the eye.

steps.text: **What did they swallow?** This changes everything you do next.
steps.warning: For ALL of them: do NOT make the person vomit. Vomiting a corrosive burns the throat twice; vomiting fuel floods the lungs.

steps.text: **Which kind of chemical?**

options.label: Corrosive — drain/oven cleaner, lye, acid, bleach, button battery
Fuel or solvent — petrol, kerosene, lighter fluid, thinner

steps.text: **Corrosives burn on the way down.** If they are **fully awake and swallowing normally**, give a few small sips of water or milk to dilute it. Wipe any chemical off the lips.
steps.warning: Do NOT make them vomit. Do NOT give acid for an alkali or alkali for an acid — it makes heat and more injury. Give nothing by mouth if they are drooling, have a hoarse/noisy voice, or trouble swallowing — that is airway swelling.
steps.detail:
- **Button/coin battery swallowed = emergency.** It burns through the gullet in as little as 2 hours. If the person is over 1 year, awake, and can swallow, give **2 teaspoons (10 mL) of honey every 10 minutes** (up to 6 doses) while you move toward help — it buffers the burn. Do not delay evacuation for it.
- Watch the airway like a hawk: drooling, a changed voice, or noisy breathing means the throat is swelling — this can close the airway.
- Activated charcoal does NOT work on corrosives — skip it.

steps.text: **Fuels and solvents are dangerous if breathed into the lungs.** Keep the person calm and upright. Rinse the mouth and spit. Do NOT induce vomiting.
steps.warning: Making them vomit fuel is worse than the swallow — even a little in the lungs causes a chemical pneumonia.
steps.detail: Watch breathing for 24 hours: coughing, fast breathing, wheeze, or blue lips means fuel reached the lungs — keep them upright, give oxygen if you have it, and evacuate. Activated charcoal does not help and raises the vomiting/aspiration risk — skip it.

steps.text: **Protect yourself — pesticide is on their skin and clothes and can poison you.** Wear gloves, remove their clothing, and wash the skin with soap and water.
steps.detail: **Organophosphate / carbamate** insecticides cause the body to flood: streaming eyes and mouth, drooling, sweating, vomiting and diarrhoea, tiny pupils, muscle twitching, and trouble breathing (remember 'SLUDGE'). This is life-threatening. The antidote is **atropine** — give it only if you have it and know how. Otherwise: decontaminate, keep the airway clear, support breathing (rescue breaths/CPR if needed), and evacuate urgently.

steps.text: **What medicine or drug was taken?** Try to find the packet — get the name, strength, how many, and when.

options.label: Opioids / sedatives — very drowsy, slow breathing

steps.text: **Note the exact drug, amount, and time.** A painkiller overdose can look fine for hours and still be deadly — this is one to take seriously and evacuate.
steps.detail:
- **Paracetamol / acetaminophen** is the quiet killer: few symptoms at first, then **liver failure over 2–4 days**. The antidote (N-acetylcysteine) works best within 8 hours and you probably don't have it — **get to medical help fast**.
- **Aspirin** overdose: ringing ears, fast deep breathing, vomiting, confusion.
- **Ibuprofen/NSAIDs**: stomach pain, later kidney trouble.
- If they are fully alert and it was within the last hour, activated charcoal (next step) can help.

steps.text: **Pinpoint pupils + slow or stopped breathing = opioid overdose. Breathing is the killer.** Give rescue breaths if breathing is inadequate. Give **naloxone** if you have it, and repeat as needed.
steps.warning: If breathing is absent or gasping, start [CPR](#/emergency/cpr). Naloxone wears off in 30–90 min and the opioid can outlast it — keep watching and re-dose.
steps.detail: Naloxone (Narcan) reverses opioids: give into the nose or muscle, repeat every 2–3 minutes until breathing returns. After it works, keep them on their side and watch — they can slip back under as it wears off. Keep breathing FOR them (rescue breaths) whenever their own breathing is too slow.

steps.text: **Put them on their side, keep them warm, and never leave them alone.** Watch breathing and be ready for vomiting.
steps.detail:
- Danger signs: slow or irregular breathing, cannot be woken, cold/clammy or blue-tinged skin, repeated vomiting, seizures. Support breathing and evacuate.
- **Cold and low blood sugar** kill drunk people — keep them warm; if they can swallow and are alert, sweet drinks help.
- **Methanol / antifreeze** (homebrew spirits, windshield fluid, some solvents) causes blindness and death and needs hospital treatment — evacuate immediately if suspected.

steps.text: **Keep a sample** of the plant, berry, or mushroom (and any vomit) to identify it. Rinse the mouth. Support them and keep them hydrated with small sips if fully alert.
steps.warning: With mushrooms, symptoms that start LATE are the dangerous ones — do not be reassured by a slow start.
steps.detail: The deadliest mushrooms (Amanita species) cause vomiting and diarrhoea **6–24 hours** after eating, then a false 'recovery', then fatal liver failure days later. Any mushroom poisoning with delayed symptoms needs urgent evacuation. Note what was eaten, when, and by whom — others may have shared it. If fully alert and it was very recent, activated charcoal (next) can help.

steps.text: **Keep the container, label, or a sample** (and any vomit). Treat supportively and watch closely — you can still manage the person even without knowing the poison.
**Do you have activated charcoal — and is it safe to give?** It only helps some poisons, and only soon after swallowing.
steps.detail: Activated charcoal binds many drugs and plant toxins if given within about an hour. It does **NOT** bind alcohols, fuels/solvents, corrosives, or metals (iron, lithium) — skip it for those. **Never** give it to a drowsy or unconscious person: they can breathe it into the lungs.

options.label: Yes — person fully alert, swallowed within ~1 hour, not a corrosive/fuel/alcohol

steps.text: **Give activated charcoal mixed in water** — about **1 gram per kilogram** of body weight (roughly 25–50 g for an adult). Only if they are fully alert and swallowing safely.

**Reassess every few minutes.** Check: are they responding, breathing normally, a good colour? Any new drowsiness, vomiting, seizures, or trouble breathing? Keep them warm and, if fully alert, sipping water.

loop.until: they are fully recovered, or you have reached medical help

steps.text: **If no help is coming.** Many serious poisonings need antidotes or a hospital. Do the things that keep a person alive: **keep the airway open, keep them breathing (rescue breaths or CPR if needed), keep them warm and hydrated, treat seizures, and save the container or sample.** Read the full document for specifics.
steps.detail: **Start moving toward help early — before a crisis — if the exposure was any of these:** paracetamol/acetaminophen, methanol or antifreeze, an organophosphate pesticide, a swallowed button battery, a corrosive, a deadly mushroom, or an opioid you can't keep reversed. Trouble breathing, airway swelling (drooling, voice change, noisy breathing), repeated seizures, or a falling level of consciousness all mean: evacuate now if it is at all possible.

protocols.title: Snakebite
protocols.entry_warning: Move away from the snake. Treat every bite as venomous.
protocols.disclaimer: If you can call your local emergency number (911 / 112), do it now. General reference only, not a substitute for trained medical care — hand over to a trained responder as soon as one is available.

steps.text: **Keep the victim calm and still.** Have them sit or lie down; carry them if possible rather than letting them walk. Panic and exertion pump venom through the body faster — your first medicine is calm.
**Immobilize the bitten limb** with a splint or sling and keep it at about **heart level**.
**Remove rings, watches, and tight clothing NOW**, before swelling makes it impossible.
**Mark the edge of the swelling** with a pen and write the time next to it. Repeat every 15–30 minutes to track spread.
**Wash the bite gently** and cover with a clean dressing.
steps.warning: Do NOT cut the wound, suck out venom, apply a tourniquet, apply ice, or give alcohol. All of these worsen the injury or speed the venom, and none remove it.

steps.text: **Is the snake a known NEUROTOXIC type** — an Australian elapid, coral snake, or similar?
**Apply a pressure-immobilization bandage**: a firm wrap of the whole limb (snug as for a sprain) plus a splint. This slows paralysing venom.
steps.warning: Do NOT use this for vipers or rattlesnakes — their venom destroys tissue, and trapping it in the limb concentrates that damage.

steps.text: **Antivenom is the only definitive treatment — evacuate to medical care**, even if symptoms seem mild; envenomation can worsen over hours. Note the snake's colour, pattern, and size **from a safe distance** (a photo is ideal); do not try to catch or kill it. Some bites inject no venom ("dry bites"), but **never assume you got one**.

protocols.title: Seizure
protocols.disclaimer: If you can call your local emergency number (911 / 112), do it now. General reference only, not a substitute for trained medical care — hand over to a trained responder as soon as one is available.

steps.text: **Protect them from injury — do not restrain them.** Clear hard or sharp objects away, or ease the person away from danger (fire, water, edges, machinery). Cushion the head with something soft.
steps.warning: Put NOTHING in the mouth. They cannot swallow their tongue; forcing anything in breaks teeth and blocks the airway.

steps.text: **Time it from the moment it starts** (or note the clock). How long it lasts decides whether this is an emergency. Loosen anything tight around the neck.
**How long has the active seizure (shaking) gone on?**

options.label: Still going at 5 minutes, or back-to-back

steps.text: **This is a life-threatening emergency (status epilepticus)** — a seizure that won't self-stop starves the brain of oxygen. Keep protecting the airway and head, and **get to help / emergency medication** any way you can. If you have prescribed rescue medication (e.g. midazolam) and know how, this is when to use it.

**When the shaking stops, roll them onto their side (recovery position)** so saliva or vomit drains. Keep the airway open and stay with them — they will be confused and drowsy ('postictal'); that is normal. Speak calmly and reassure them.
**Do any of these apply?** They change this from 'wait it out' to 'reach help'.

options.label: First-ever seizure, injury, in water, pregnant, or not waking

steps.text: **Reach help / evacuate.** A first-ever seizure, one causing injury, one in water, one in a pregnant woman, repeated seizures without waking between, or failure to wake within ~15–20 minutes all need medical assessment.

**Treat the cause you can find (see the full document):** cool a feverish child gradually; give sugar if low blood sugar and they are fully awake; rehydrate with ORS (not plain water) after heavy sweating; consider head injury, and alcohol or medication withdrawal when supplies run out.
steps.detail:
- **Febrile seizure (young child, fever):** cool gradually — remove layers, lukewarm sponging — never plunge into cold water; treat the fever. Usually brief and not dangerous, but a first one should be checked.
- **Low blood sugar** (diabetic): once fully awake and able to swallow, give sugar (see the Diabetic emergency guide).
- **Low salt** from drinking only water after heavy sweating — rehydrate with oral rehydration solution.
- **Withdrawal** from alcohol or seizure medicines running out can trigger dangerous repeated seizures — evacuate.
- **Recurrent seizures off-grid:** protect during each, keep them safe and hydrated, ration any remaining medication sensibly, and move toward help.

protocols.entry_warning: Protect them and do NOT restrain or put anything in the mouth.

protocols.title: Drowning
protocols.entry_warning: Rescue from shore — do not become the second victim.
protocols.disclaimer: If you can call your local emergency number (911 / 112), do it now. General reference only, not a substitute for trained medical care — hand over to a trained responder as soon as one is available.

steps.text: **Reach, throw, row — don't go.** Rescue from shore first: **reach** with a stick, paddle, or clothing; **throw** a rope or anything that floats; **row** a boat out. Enter the water only as a last resort, and bring something that floats.
steps.warning: A drowning person will climb on you and push you under. More would-be rescuers drown than victims. Do not become the second casualty.

steps.text: **Get them out and onto their back on firm ground.** Keep them horizontal — hauling a near-drowning person upright can drop their blood pressure. Then check breathing.
steps.detail: If they fell or dived and a neck injury is possible, keep the head and neck as still and aligned as you can while you move them — but **an open airway and breathing always come first**; don't let spinal caution stop you resuscitating.

steps.text: **Check for normal breathing — no more than 10 seconds.** Gasping is NOT breathing. What do you see?
**Put them on their side (recovery position)** so water and vomit drain, keep them warm, and watch breathing continuously — it can fail again.

**Give 5 initial rescue breaths, THEN start compressions.** Drowning arrest is from lack of oxygen, so breaths come first — the opposite of a normal adult collapse. Tilt the head, lift the chin, seal your mouth over theirs (mouth and nose for a small child).
**Continue CPR at 30 compressions to 2 breaths.** Follow the full [CPR protocol](#/emergency/cpr). Keep the breaths in — do not switch to hands-only for a drowning victim.

**Expect vomiting** — it happens in most drowning resuscitations. **Roll them onto their side, sweep the mouth clear, then roll back and continue.** Do NOT waste time trying to 'drain the lungs' by pressing the belly or hanging them upside down — it doesn't work and delays CPR.

**Cold-water victims are usually hypothermic — this can save them.** Handle very gently, remove wet clothing, insulate above and below, and warm gradually. The cold protects the brain, so **keep resuscitating far longer than usual: 'not dead until warm and dead.'**

**Watch for 24 hours, even if they seem fine.** Anyone who inhaled water can deteriorate hours later ('secondary drowning'): worsening cough, fast or laboured breathing, chest tightness, or unusual tiredness. Treat new breathing trouble as an emergency and evacuate.

protocols.title: Heat Exhaustion and Heat Stroke
protocols.entry_warning: The dividing line is the brain: confusion means heat stroke.
protocols.disclaimer: If you can call your local emergency number (911 / 112), do it now. General reference only, not a substitute for trained medical care — hand over to a trained responder as soon as one is available.

steps.text: **What are you seeing?** Heat illness runs cramps → exhaustion → stroke. The dividing line is the brain: **confusion or collapse means heat STROKE.**

options.label: Confused, stumbling, collapsed, or seizing — hot skin
Sweating, weak, nauseous, dizzy — but thinking clearly

steps.text: **HEAT STROKE — cool aggressively NOW. Cool first, move second.** Get them into shade, strip off clothing. Every minute hot damages the brain and organs.
steps.warning: Confusion, hot skin (which may be dry OR still sweaty), or collapse in the heat is life-threatening.

steps.text: **How can you cool them?**

options.label: Water to immerse them (stream, tub, trough)

steps.text: **Whole-body immersion in cool water is the best treatment.** Lower them into a stream, trough, or tub, **keeping the head supported above water**. Keep them in until they cool.
**Douse continuously with water and fan hard** to drive evaporation. Drape a wet sheet and fan it; put **cold packs / wet cloths at the neck, armpits, and groin**. Keep re-wetting.

**Stop active cooling once they become alert** (avoid overshooting into shivering cold). Shivering alone is not a reason to stop — heat-stroke victims often shiver while still dangerously hot. **Give nothing by mouth while confused.** Heat stroke damages organs — evacuate to care.
**Heat cramps: rest in shade, gently stretch the muscle, and replace salt and water** — sip oral rehydration solution or lightly salted water. Do not go back to hard work until the cramps settle.
**Heat exhaustion: stop all work now.** Move to shade and loosen or remove clothing.
**Lie them flat with the legs raised.** Cool the skin with a wet cloth and fanning.
**Sip water or oral rehydration solution steadily** — about half a litre over 15–20 minutes, with electrolytes/salt (plain water alone for hours risks dangerously low sodium).
**Recovering within about 30 minutes?**

options.label: No, or the mind is clouding

steps.text: **Rest in the shade and keep hydrating.** No return to hard work in the heat today. Off-grid prevention: work at dawn and dusk, acclimatise over 1–2 weeks, drink ~0.5–1 L/hour with salt during hard work, and check urine is pale straw.

protocols.title: Hypothermia and Frostbite
protocols.disclaimer: If you can call your local emergency number (911 / 112), do it now. General reference only, not a substitute for trained medical care — hand over to a trained responder as soon as one is available.

steps.text: **What are you dealing with?**

options.label: Whole body cold — shivering, clumsy, confused
A body part — numb, white, hard (frostbite)
Wet, cold feet for hours — not frozen (trench foot)

steps.text: **Hypothermia: get them into shelter, out of the wind, and off the cold ground.** Remove wet clothing (cut it away to avoid moving them much) and insulate all around — including the head and neck — above and below.
**How bad is it?**

options.label: Shivering stopped, drowsy/confused, or barely rousable (severe)

steps.text: **Rewarm gently.** Add dry insulating layers, cover the head, and apply gentle warmth to the core (chest, neck, armpits, groin). If **fully alert** and able to swallow, give **warm sweet drinks** — no alcohol. Let them move gently to make heat.

**Severe hypothermia — handle very gently.** Rough movement can stop a cold heart. Insulate thoroughly and add gentle heat to the core only; do NOT rub the limbs or use fierce heat, and give **nothing by mouth**. If they are not breathing, start CPR and keep going a long time — **'not dead until warm and dead.'** Evacuate.

**Rewarm slowly and keep watching.** Warming can cause 'afterdrop' — cold blood from the limbs cooling the core — so warm the core first and keep the person still and insulated. Keep them dry and sheltered until steadily improving.
**Frostbite: get out of the cold. Remove rings, watches, and wet clothing** before swelling starts. **Do NOT rub the part** (ice crystals tear tissue) and do not use direct fierce heat like a fire.
**Could the part refreeze after you thaw it?** Refreezing destroys tissue far worse than staying frozen — this decision matters.

options.label: Yes — still in the cold, or must travel out
No — safe and warm to stay

steps.text: **Do NOT rewarm yet.** Keep the part frozen, padded, and protected, and get to safety. It is safer to **walk out on a frozen foot** than to thaw it and have it refreeze — a thawed part cannot be walked on and must not refreeze.

**Rewarm in warm water at 37–39 °C (99–102 °F)** — comfortably warm to your elbow, not hot — for **20–40 minutes**, until the part is soft and flushed pink/red. It will hurt a lot as it thaws; that pain means living tissue. Give pain relief.
steps.warning: Never thaw with a fire, stove, or hot rock — numb skin burns without you feeling it.

steps.text: **Leave blisters intact.** Place dry gauze between thawed fingers or toes, keep the part elevated, clean, and protected, and **do not walk on a thawed foot**. Watch for infection over the following days. Evacuate — depth of damage takes weeks to declare and needs care.

**Trench/immersion foot** (wet + cold above freezing for hours to days): get the feet **dry and warm gradually**, elevate them, and do **not** rub or walk on them. Keep them dry from now on. Expect burning pain as they warm; watch for infection. Prevent it by keeping feet dry and changing wet socks.

protocols.entry_warning: Loss of shivering with drowsiness is severe hypothermia — handle gently.

protocols.title: Emergency Childbirth
protocols.disclaimer: If you can call your local emergency number (911 / 112), do it now. General reference only, not a substitute for trained medical care — hand over to a trained responder as soon as one is available.

steps.text: **Birth is usually normal — stay calm and let it happen.** Wash your hands well, lay out clean cloths or plastic, and reassure the mother. Do not rush or pull.
**Is the birth imminent?** (An overwhelming urge to push, or the baby's head bulging/visible.)

options.label: Yes — head coming / strong urge to push

steps.text: **Let labour progress at its own pace.** Time the contractions; let her move and find comfortable positions; **do not push before the urge comes.** Get to skilled help if you possibly can — early labour leaves time to move. Keep her hydrated and calm.
**Let her push with the contractions.** As the head crowns, **support it gently** with a clean hand so it doesn't deliver too fast — do NOT pull. Let the head turn on its own.
**Is the cord looped around the baby's neck?** Feel gently after the head is out.
**Gently hook a finger under the cord and slip the loop over the baby's head.** If it is too tight to move, keep the baby close and deliver quickly — the body will come with the next push.

**Deliver the shoulders.** With the next contraction, guide the head **gently downward** to free the top shoulder, then **gently upward** for the lower one. The body follows fast and slippery — support it.
steps.detail: **Shoulders stuck after the head is out (shoulder dystocia)?** Get the mother's **knees up to her chest** (McRoberts) and have someone press just above the pubic bone. Do NOT pull hard on the head. **Baby coming feet- or bottom-first (breech)?** Let it deliver on its own, support the body, and do not pull — keep the body warm and wait for the head; if the head delays, lift the body slightly and clear the baby's mouth.

steps.text: **Dry the baby vigorously** with a clean cloth and place it **skin-to-skin on the mother's bare chest**, covering both — especially the baby's head. Note the time of birth. Drying and warmth are usually all a baby needs to start breathing.
**Is the baby crying or breathing well?** Most do within a minute of drying.

**Stimulate, then breathe for the baby.** Rub the back firmly and flick the soles; wipe the mouth and nose clear. If still not breathing after about a minute, give **gentle puffs of air** (just enough to lift the chest, about 30–40/min).
steps.detail: If there is still no breathing or heartbeat after good inflation breaths, start infant CPR at **3 compressions to 1 breath** (two thumbs on the breastbone). Keep the baby warm and dry throughout — cold babies don't respond. See *CPR for Adults, Children, and Infants*.

steps.text: **Do not cut the cord early.** Leave it alone — wait until it **stops pulsing** (several minutes), which lets the baby get its full blood. Then tie it tightly in two places and cut between them with a clean, flame-sterilised blade.

**The placenta usually delivers within 5–30 minutes.** Let it come with a gentle push from the mother — **do NOT pull the cord** (it can turn the womb inside out). Keep the placenta; check it looks complete.

**Prevent bleeding: after the placenta is out, massage the womb.** Rub the lower belly firmly until you feel it harden into a ball, and **put the baby to the breast** — both make the womb clamp down and stop bleeding.
steps.warning: Heavy bleeding after birth is the biggest killer of mothers. Keep massaging the womb firm, keep her flat and warm, and treat for shock.
steps.detail: Off-grid postpartum haemorrhage: keep the uterus massaged firm, keep the baby suckling, lay her flat with legs raised, keep her warm, and give fluids by mouth if fully alert — and evacuate urgently. If you have and know how to use **misoprostol or oxytocin**, this is when it is given.

steps.text: **Danger signs — get skilled help / evacuate:** heavy bleeding; a **foot, arm, or cord coming before the head**; the placenta not delivering within ~30–60 minutes; the baby not breathing well; or the mother becoming faint, feverish, or very unwell. Keep mother and baby warm, together, and monitored.

protocols.entry_warning: Birth is usually normal. Stay calm, keep it clean, do not pull.
